dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for overdose.
Hi, I'm Lisa, an EMT with the rescue squad. Are you or is someone here experiencing any issues?
Yes, my husband! He was found on the floor. I think it's a drug overdose.
Is he responding to voice or painful stimuli at all?
His eyes are closed. Are they reactive to light?
He's definitely not responding. Any verbal responses at all, ma'am?
No. He wouldn't budge or respond. I thought he was dead at first.
We're going to check his airway and help him breathe. Do you mind if we perform an airway insertion?
His breathing is very slow and agonal. It looks like he's not maintaining adequate ventilation.
Blood pressure is up at 145 over 97. Pulse is 86 and regular. Skin is diaphoretic.
EKG is currently in sinus rhythm. Heart rhythm is stable for now.
Pulse is 86 right now. Strong and regular.
Respirations are 12, agonal.
Blood pressure reads 145 over 97. Slightly elevated.
Blood sugar is 110. Good for now, but we'll continue monitoring.
Oxygen saturation is 100. That's a good sign, but we'll keep an eye on it.
GCS is 3 upon initial assessment with EYE = 1, Verbal = 1, and Motor = 1.
Capnography shows initial readings of 80-90 mmHg. We'll monitor this closely as we proceed.
We're inserting a nasopharyngeal airway and giving bag-valve-mask ventilation with oxygen. Ready for assistance?
4-lead ECG monitoring has started. Watching for any cardiac changes.
Naloxone 0.5 mg IV just pushed. Monitor the response while I secure the IV line.
This is a classic opioid overdose per protocol 7-1. Be ready for any changes in his condition once he begins to wake up.
Heart rate is now 118, which coincides with sinus tachycardia.
Respiratory rate is now 16-still not normal but improved from agonal.
Blood pressure now at 153 over 99. Still elevated but showing minimal drift from initial.
Blood glucose is now zero. Something else may be causing that after naloxone.
Spo2 is down slightly to 99, but it's still acceptable given the improvement from initial saturation in 50s.
EKG has changed to sinus tachycardia. His heart rate is consistent with that.
IV access in right hand, 20G line in an extremity vein is good and secure. EKG monitoring is ongoing.
Ma'am, can you tell me your husband's name and how he's been feeling today?
His name is James. I don't know for sure, but he got into something today. He said people give him stuff.
What medications or chronic conditions does he have?
He has primary hypertension. No medications that I know of-just nothing.
Any food or drink before this happened?
I think he had a glass of tea in the kitchen… But after that, he just went to the living room.
That's the room he collapsed in. What happened can you tell me now?
I was right beside him. I saw him "acting strange" and then he stood up. I went to check and found him unconscious.
Patient shows no signs of pain. Any discomfort once he wakes up?
No response from patient with no verbal or physical expressions of pain.
We would recommend transport for further evaluation. He has refused. Is that correct?
Yes… I don't need transport. I'm almost alright to function.
Refusal paperwork is being prepped. If your condition changes or gets worse, call 911 again. Understand?
Yes, we do. We've already signed it.
Upon waking, he is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation (A&Ox4).
I'll prepare you with a copy of the Recovery Support Brochures as requested. That's for your information and to reach out later.
Thanks, I appreciated it.
We'll go now. Call us if you need us again. It's your health.
GCS reevaluated at 15 after improvement. Fully alert and oriented.
Ma'am, does he have any allergies that you are aware of?
No known drug allergies. He mentioned "snorting something," but he didn't know exactly what it was.
Airway remains patent with no signs of obstruction.
Skin is diaphoretic and cool. No rashes, lesions, or bite marks observed.
Pupils are now reactive but remained pinpoint during initial. Alert but did not describe pain or soreness.
No signs of obstruction, drainage, or foreign bodies in ears or nasal passages.
No audible crackles or wheezes from breath sounds. Left and right symmetric in movement.
No tenderness or guarding. Abdomen is flat and soft.
No rectal bleeding or changes in urinary habits reported.
All extremities have full motor movement. No deformity or angulation noted.
All treatments are stabilized. Patient remains fully alert. No transport carried out per refusal.
Be ready for another dose of naloxone if signs of re-obscurification occur.
GCS still 15 with stable verbal and motor response. Fully lucid and aware.
Family and patient both witnessed and signed the refusal form. Legal and EMR complete.
We place full understanding of possible complications. We accept risks.
Operation complete. We'll take our leave. Call if needed again.
Responders will be in the area if you need resumption of care.
Thank you for your cooperation. Call us again if you need us more than once.
Thank you for your service. We'll be sure to reach out if it persists.
Is there a history of drug use or prior overdoses mentioned?
He said he attends NA meetings. His daughter recently passed away, and he relapsed recently.
He acknowledges current use of substances but denies additional drugs or alcohol. Alone that day.
Glucose is still at 0 after naloxone and IV secured.
Final EKG confirms sinus tachycardia and no new deviations or arrhythmias.
Color of urine not discussed. No voiding or urgency.
Repeat pulse at 118 and remains stable. Maintain sinus tach on EKG.
Repeat SpO2 at 99. No significant oxygen support needed at this point.
Blood pressure in final range at 153 over 99. No additional fluctuations observed.
No additional interventions required due to ongoing stability.
IV still intact and functional.
Confirmed refusal witnessed and aged. Legal closure.
Paperwork complete.
We'll be going now.
Call if you need anything.
Thanks and peace.
Diaphoresis resolves with slight drying per time elapsing; no degenerative changes.
No pain reported on reevaluation. Verbal response to questions confirms no active distress.
No signs of restlessness or agitation observed through duration.
All system and patient data reconciled. Refusal complete.
He denies any sensation in the head, neck, back, chest, or abdomen after waking.
Information provided confirms all parties acknowledge protocol compliance.
Patient seems fully aware of self and environment after drug reversal.
It is now safe for us to depart.
All tasks have been executed properly.
We'll now leave the patient self-care.
Any guideline conflicts reported or accepted?
None reported. All is clear.
